prompt:
The Élan case study Section 2 reads as a chain of sentence
fragments. "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." Three
sentences of three words each, back to back. It's punchy in
isolation but four paragraphs in a row of this structure becomes
exhausting. Fix the rhythm and tell me when fragment chains earn
their place vs. when they're a tic.
response:
I have a complete picture. I don't need the live CMS seed file verbatim to diagnose and fix this - the dossier, feedback log, CAP-027 node, and voice docs give me everything. Let me now construct the full, precise response.

---

## Diagnosis

**Anti-pattern triggered: CAP-027 — Fragment Chain as Default Prose**

This isn't a new finding. The Frustration Log in `docs/content/projects/engram.md` records this exact failure mode being fixed once already (2026-04-06, "machine-generated short sentences that don't make sense"), and CAP-027 was created from that session. It has reasserted itself in Section 2's current draft - the fix didn't hold because the replacement copy still leans on fragments as its structural default, just at paragraph scale instead of sentence scale.

---

## Root Cause

**CAP-027: Fragment Chain as Default Prose.** Three compounding mechanics are at work simultaneously:

**1. Fragments used as the spine, not the punchline.**
`voice-style.md §13.1` is unambiguous: *"Use sentence fragments to sharpen a point. [...] Fragment density by zone: in feature section bodies (Zone 3), cap at 2-3 fragments per section."* Section 2 has four consecutive paragraphs, each structured as a 3-word subject-dropping fragment. That's 4–8+ fragments in a single Zone 3 section, blowing the cap by 2-4x.

The rule isn't "no fragments." It's that a fragment is a punchline. A punchline requires a setup sentence to land against. When every sentence is a punchline, the contrast disappears and the rhythm flattens into what the Frustration Log called "telegram staccato."

**2. No cause-and-consequence connective tissue.**
The Engram dossier's key positive signal: *"section bodies need narrative sentences with cause-and-consequence, not telegraphic lists."* Fragment chains structurally forbid cause-and-consequence — they strip the verbs, conjunctions, and logical connectors that show *why* one thing led to another. The reader gets a sequence of assertions with no causal reasoning binding them. That's a seniority-signal problem: juniors list what happened; seniors explain why it happened that way.

**3. Cognitive load from parallel structure overload.**
When four paragraphs run the same NVA → NVA → NVA rhythm, the reader's pattern-recognition fires and disengages. The prose stops feeling like a designer's thinking and starts feeling like a bulleted list wearing a disguise.

---

## The Fix

**File:** `src/app/api/seed/update-engram/route.ts` (or wherever Section 2 body copy lives as a string constant)

The principle: each paragraph needs exactly one **full narrative sentence** that carries the cause-and-consequence load, with at most one **fragment** as a following beat. Only the last paragraph of the section earns a pure fragment chain as a rhetorical landing — because by that point you've built enough setup for the staccato to hit.

Here is the rewrite for Section 2 ("The System Behind the System"), working with the structure described in the dossier (SkillMap visual: 18 skills by phase + 200+ anti-patterns as proportional blocks):

---

**Before (representative current state):**
> Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps.
>
> Eighteen skills. Each one a different operation. Each one a phase.
>
> Two hundred patterns. Named. Enforced.
>
> Not a library. A control layer.

**After:**
> One correction, uncaptured, costs nothing. The fiftieth copy of the same mistake costs the entire session - and by then you've already shipped the wrong thing. The system exists to stop that compounding.
>
> Eighteen skills route every feedback type to the right document: design corrections to anti-patterns, engineering decisions to guardrails, voice drift to the voice register. No judgment call at the point of capture.
>
> Two hundred anti-patterns encode what not to repeat. The proportional blocks in the Knowledge view show which domains produce the most errors - a map of where the system is still learning.
>
> Not a library. A control layer.

---

**What changed and why, paragraph by paragraph:**

| ¶ | Before | After | Why |
|---|--------|-------|-----|
| 1 | `Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps.` | Full sentence with cause-and-consequence (`costs nothing` → `costs the session` → `already shipped`) + Escalation Beat (T4). | Sets up the *reason* the system exists before showing what it is. The fragment chain started with a conclusion; this starts with the argument. |
| 2 | `Eighteen skills. Each one a different operation. Each one a phase.` | One full sentence with two embedded specifics (routing + document type). | Adds the *mechanism* — how the 18 skills actually work — instead of just counting them. The SkillMap shows the routing; the text names it. |
| 3 | `Two hundred patterns. Named. Enforced.` | Two sentences: count + what the visual shows. | "Named. Enforced." is fine as a standalone — but the visual already *shows* named and enforced. The text needs to say what the visual can't: which domains are the error-dense ones. |
| 4 | `Not a library. A control layer.` | Unchanged. | This earns its fragment chain. It's a single two-beat verdict at the end, not a default mode — it lands because the three paragraphs before it were full sentences. The contrast is the point. |

---

## When Fragment Chains Earn Their Place vs. When They're a Tic

This is the judgment call the docs encode but don't state as a single heuristic. Here it is:

### A fragment chain earns its place when it functions as a **contrast beat**

A fragment needs something full to push against. The rhythm mechanism is: *long thought → short landing.* The short sentence hits harder because the long one set it up.

```
// ✅ EARNED — contrast is doing work
The agent had every correction available. Every pattern documented.
Every rule enforced at the moment of generation.
It still drifted. Every single session.

// ✅ EARNED — verdict after argument (your "Not a library. A control layer.")
Eighteen skills route corrections. Two hundred patterns encode the past.
The system doesn't make the agent smarter.
Not smarter. More constrained.

// ✅ EARNED — Zone 1 (blurb), where fragments carry rhythm freely per voice-style.md §13.1
AI powerful? Yes. AI dumb? Also yes.
```

### A fragment chain is a tic when it functions as **default prose rhythm**

The tell is: *could you add a verb and conjunction to every fragment and get a richer sentence?* If yes, the fragment is suppressing information rather than compressing it.

```
// ❌ TIC — every sentence is a fragment, no contrast, information suppressed
Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps.
Eighteen skills. Each one a different operation. Each one a phase.
Two hundred patterns. Named. Enforced.

// ❌ TIC — parallel structure × 4 paragraphs = telegram mode
Researched the problem. Found the pattern. Fixed the rule.
Tested the output. Saw improvement. Shipped the update.
```

The operational test — **three questions before you write a fragment:**

1. **Is there a full sentence before this that it lands against?** If not, write the full sentence first, then the fragment.
2. **Does the fragment compress information, or does it suppress it?** "Not smarter. More constrained." compresses a nuanced distinction into 4 words. "Eighteen skills. Each one a phase." suppresses: *what do the skills actually do?*
3. **Is this Zone 1 (blurb) or Zone 3 (section body)?** Zone 1 = fragments can be the rhythm. Zone 3 = max 2-3 fragments for the whole section, used as punchlines only.

The Engram Frustration Log entry from 2026-04-06 nails the underlying rule concisely and is worth keeping as a mental model: *"Fragments work as punchlines after setup but not as the default prose rhythm. Benchmark: Lacework and Meteor section bodies tell mini-stories."* Every section body should contain at least one mini-story — a cause, a consequence, and a detail specific enough that it couldn't have come from a different project.

---

## Principle

**Fragment = punchline, not spine.** A fragment chain draws its force from contrast with the full sentence before it. Four paragraphs of fragments don't have four punchlines — they have zero, because the contrast that makes a fragment land has been removed. The Zone 3 cap (2–3 fragments per section, per `voice-style.md §13.1`) isn't arbitrary: it's the maximum before the contrast disappears and the rhythm flattens. The one place to defy this is the very last beat of a section, where a verdict fragment earns its staccato by arriving after three full sentences of argument.
